notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Attorney General; Minister to Britain

narrative_blocks.header: Attorney General who prosecuted the Whiskey Ring
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed Attorney General by President Grant in 1875, he prosecuted the Whiskey Ring tax-evasion syndicate and obtained 110 convictions. He confronted Grant directly when the President's own secretary Orville Babcock was implicated.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecuted Lincoln assassination conspirator John Surratt
narrative_blocks.body: In 1867 he was hired by the federal government to prosecute John H. Surratt, an alleged conspirator in Lincoln's assassination. His closing argument famously compared Surratt's testimony to 'lies generating perjuries… like flies in a rotting carcass' — but Surratt was acquitted by a hung jury.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped overthrow the Tweed Ring in 1871
narrative_blocks.body: As a leading member of New York's Committee of Seventy, he helped overthrow the Tammany Hall Tweed Ring in 1871. He was then serving as U.S. District Attorney for the Southern District of New York under Grant's appointment.

narrative_blocks.header: Introduced former President Grant to Queen Victoria
narrative_blocks.body: As U.S. Minister to Great Britain (1876-1877) he argued successfully that Grant deserved royal precedence as a former head of state. 'Once a President always a President,' he said — and introduced Grant to Queen Victoria at Windsor Castle.

narrative_blocks.header: Phrenologist who wanted his bust enlarged like Plato
narrative_blocks.body: In 1874 he commissioned Augustus Saint-Gaudens for a marble bust and kept asking him to enlarge his head to resemble Socrates and Plato. Saint-Gaudens wrote that the finished bust 'seemed to be affected with some dreadful swelling disease.'

education.notes: — Yale 1837; Yale Law School; Yale honorary Doctor of Laws 1873; Oxford D.C.L. 1878. Attorney General under Grant; Minister to Court of St. James.

notable.notable_blurb: Merchant, US Commissioner to Paris Exposition 1867

narrative_blocks.header: America’s commissioner at the Paris Exposition
narrative_blocks.body: The merchant served as a United States Commissioner to the Paris Exposition of 1867.

cross_connections.display_label: The Pierpont land owned by Capt. Giles Pierpont Jr. in.
The Pierpont land owned by Capt. Giles Pierpont Jr. in.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Laura died days after him
narrative_blocks.body: He died December 28, 1842 at age 59 in North Haven. His sixteen-year-old daughter Laura died a few days later, leaving the family doubly bereft in the same week.

narrative_blocks.header: His Pierpont land became Clover Dairy Farm
narrative_blocks.body: The land he inherited had passed from Rev. James Pierpont to Lt. Joseph Pierpont to his father Giles to him — a four-generation Pierpont landholding. After his death it was sold and became Clover Dairy Farm under Dr. D.W. Patten.

narrative_blocks.header: Son became U.S. Attorney General under Grant
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest son was baptized Munson Edwards Pierpont in 1813 — by first wife Eunice Munson, who died eight months later. The son later dropped 'Munson' and changed his surname to Pierrepont, serving as U.S. Attorney General under President Grant in 1875.

cross_connections.display_label: His paternal aunt — Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont
His Pierpont grandnephew became U.S. Attorney General under Grant

narrative_blocks.header: Wealthiest man in North Haven in 1786
narrative_blocks.body: He was 'accounted to be the wealthiest man in North Haven, Connecticut, in 1786' at age 45, having served as a Sergeant in the Connecticut Militia. He held land that had passed through three generations: from Rev. James Pierpont to son Lt. Joseph Pierpont to him.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife outlived him by nine months
narrative_blocks.body: He died January 16, 1832 at age 90; his wife Elizabeth Cooper Pierpont died November 1, 1832 at age 88. After 66 years of marriage, she survived him by nine months.

narrative_blocks.header: His Pierpont land became 'Dr. Patten place'
narrative_blocks.body: The Pierpont tract eventually passed to his son Capt. Giles Jr., father of Munson Edward Pierpont — described by Thorpe as a distinguished jurist and diplomat. The land later became known locally as 'Dr. Patten place,' marking the end of a four-generation Pierpont landholding.

INST010.name: New Haven Law School
INST010.notes: The New Haven Law School, affiliated with Yale. Edwards Pierrepont graduated 1840. Now integrated as Yale Law School.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
